summary: DHH celebrates the end of easy-money funding conditions as interest rates rise and recession looms. He argues that cheap capital enabled a culture of short-termism, inflated valuations, and reckless cash-burning while ignoring business fundamentals like profitability. Startups justified endless losses by invoking Amazon, Facebook, and Google as precedents, despite the statistical improbability of replicating their outcomes. DHH sees the downturn as a healthy correction that forces entrepreneurs to learn sustainable business basics.
key_points: Rising interest rates and recession fears are ending the era of easy venture funding on soft terms
The cheap-money era enabled scams, cash-burning, and obsession with valuation over actual profitability
Startups justified perpetual losses by pointing to Amazon/Facebook/Google, ignoring survivorship bias
Economic downturns force entrepreneurs to learn fundamentals they ignored during boom times
Both dreamers and realists are needed — but the realist perspective has been suppressed during The Good Times
money_quotes: The Good Times™ all too often serve as an enabling backdrop for short-termism and hot potatoes.
It is most definitely, absolutely, not the time to talk about the boring fundamentals of business.
The Good Times™ teach entrepreneurs that the only thing that really matters is inflating the fantasy figure known as valuation.
Companies that wandered in the desert of unprofitability for longer than Moses, but eventually found the holy land of monopoly rents.
Just jam the pipeline full of more dreams, and surely it'll happen!
When the gravy gets spooked, and you have to get by on your own cooked cabbage until you can afford better, that's when you learn the basics.
It's good to dream. But it's also good to wake up.
key_insight: Economic downturns are healthy corrections that force startups to abandon valuation theater and relearn the fundamentals of building profitable businesses.
